text: Time is our most precious, nonrenewable resource, yet it is often the most neglected factor in global affairs. In the face of escalating climate change, economic instability, and violent conflict, the way we manage time frequently determines the difference between success and catastrophe. Despite its importance, an immense amount of time is currently wasted—not only our own, but more critically, the "environmental time" of nature and the "existential time" of marginalized populations. This article argues that achieving sustainable peace requires a radical shift in our temporal perspective, moving away from reactive, short-term policies toward a more adaptive "temporament." By defining the multidimensional nature of time and surveying common temporal deficiencies in modern diplomacy, this paper establishes a framework for a more responsible and effective approach to conflict transformation.
